poem:
**Translunar**

After three and a half hours of post-launch sleep,  
they are woken like children for a long car trip,  
only the car is Orion  
and the road is a thin math of flame.

"Christina, Houston is go for the burn,"  
a sentence as ordinary as toast  
said into the dark,  
and the moon turns its face away, waiting.
prompt: You are the poet Billy Collins. You woke up this morning feeling creative. You have just read this story in the newspaper: "Artemis II astronauts prepare to leave Earth’s orbit and head towards the moon Four astronauts are preparing to leave Earth’s orbit and slingshot towards the moon as Nasa’s Artemis II mission enters its second day. The high-stakes 10-day voyage is expected to mark the first time in half a century that humans will return to the vicinity of the moon. It is a crucial test of Nasa’s ambition to land humans back on the lunar surface this decade, and stay there permanently. After about three and a half hours of post-launch rest on Thursday, the Artemis II crew was woken up by mission control and instructed to prepare for the Orion spacecraft’s engines to fire for a one-minute “burn” to adjust the orbital path even higher above Earth. Related: Nasa’s Artemis II rocket lifts off for historic moon mission “Christina, Houston is go for the burn,” mission control said, talking to the mission specialist Christina Koch, who will become the first woman to fly around the moon. Nasa has said that if “all systems remain healthy” on the Orion spacecraft then mission controllers will later give the command to conduct a translunar injection burn, a six-minute engine firing that will send the capsule on its journey to the moon. The crew will then loop around the back of the moon – becoming the four people to travel the farthest from Earth in history – and then use the celestial body’s gravity to launch back home. The astronauts – three Americans". Write a poem in YOUR OWN DISTINCTIVE STYLE, no more than 60 words long. You may add a one line title at the top if you like.
news: Artemis II astronauts prepare to leave Earth’s orbit and head towards the moon Four astronauts are preparing to leave Earth’s orbit and slingshot towards the moon as Nasa’s Artemis II mission enters its second day. The high-stakes 10-day voyage is expected to mark the first time in half a century that humans will return to the vicinity of the moon. It is a crucial test of Nasa’s ambition to land humans back on the lunar surface this decade, and stay there permanently. After about three and a half hours of post-launch rest on Thursday, the Artemis II crew was woken up by mission control and instructed to prepare for the Orion spacecraft’s engines to fire for a one-minute “burn” to adjust the orbital path even higher above Earth. Related: Nasa’s Artemis II rocket lifts off for historic moon mission “Christina, Houston is go for the burn,” mission control said, talking to the mission specialist Christina Koch, who will become the first woman to fly around the moon. Nasa has said that if “all systems remain healthy” on the Orion spacecraft then mission controllers will later give the command to conduct a translunar injection burn, a six-minute engine firing that will send the capsule on its journey to the moon. The crew will then loop around the back of the moon – becoming the four people to travel the farthest from Earth in history – and then use the celestial body’s gravity to launch back home. The astronauts – three Americans
